1. Get Richer Talk with Jeremy Haynes.mp4
The theme of this specific Get Richer talk, I thought about this deeply, and I like to pick on a few of you guys every time that I consider one of these talks, as it inspires me more to be more specific and articulate to you about the specific problems you deal with as a group. So. I did a talk recently at one of the Inner Circle students' masterminds. And it really pissed me off. I was very angry. I get very frustrated when people think that they've made enough. and they get comfortable and complacent and they plateau at particular levels financially in their lives. And it generally comes from the fact that they think that they've become big shit popping— they've made enough to do some stuff that they previously couldn't do.
And it's sad to see, every single time, it's sad to see, because it immediately leads to contraction months later, or sometimes years later. And it happens at every financial level. But in this particular talk that I did for this mastermind, all these guys, they were fucking super broke. They were making 50K a month to max. Three of them were making 100K a month. None of them had more than 500K. All of them had $500,000 or less in equities, assets, cash in their accounts, money that they could access. None of them had more than $500,000. All of them far, far less. None of them took investing seriously because they couldn't— because they were making a measly fucking $50,000, $100,000 a month. $100,000 a month, to be clear, $1.
2 million a year. Give or take where you live and how fuck do you get from a tax perspective, we all know that's net a good couple hundred grand tops. And yet every single one of these guys, one of them in particular— what's that fucking wiry kid's name, Chris? The nerd from Germany. Fuck that guy. This guy, after the event, after I sit here and grill these guys on getting richer, he goes on YouTube, tells me I'm getting fatter and fatter and that I have gray hair and just dismisses everything I told him because I grilled him so fucking hard. He was making net like 600 grand a year tops. And he genuinely thought that everything I said to him was from a perspective of disrespect instead of a perspective of pushing him to a greater level financially in his life.
He's the kind of person that ends up in a position... Where he's all of a sudden late 30s, all of a sudden late 40s, all of a sudden in his 50s, has barely anything in his investment account. His skills are all old, antiquated. Young kids like you guys are coming in and sweeping him in terms of revenue. Antiquated, doesn't have the drive anymore to adapt, doesn't have the drive to get new skills. And he ends up in a fucked position financially in his life where he has to settle. Becomes one of those old people who lives in an A-frame house, maybe works at a grocery store to justify being social, but in reality it's because he needs the money and he can't support himself off social security income and the puny little pipsqueak investment portfolio he managed to build throughout his career.
And then he realizes. Know what Jeremy said back in the day. I should have been fucking doing that. That's when he learns the value of the lessons. So anyway. Inspired by that specific talk. I thought of a few of you guys, you know, believe it or not, the cool thing about this room now, almost more than 60% of you guys are doing that a floor, like a hundred K a month. Some of you guys are up. Of even a million plus a month now it's tremendous to see some of you guys are still stuck in that range of like 20 to 50k a month and this specific set of lessons today are applicable to all of you. You guys ready to go? I ask you a simple question. What challenges you?
To go out there. And push yourself substantially harder than you are now. When's the last time that you tapped in to the drive you had when you were poor? Do you remember it? Think about it back in the day. How much more work you did in a day. Think about how much more risk you took. Think about how driven every single fucking day of your life you were when you woke up. And you went out there and you worked hard for whatever it was that you were trying to achieve. Likely a small, puny goal that would make you cry if you made that amount of money today. Cry in a bad way, not a good way. You lost that drive. Most people that I talk to.
That reach a point financially where they're at that level where they start to kind of plateau and they don't see themselves continuing the momentum at the pace they did that got them to that point. They all agree on one simple thing. They just don't do as much as they historically did. They're typically quiet about it. It's internal talk. You can see it in the numbers if you're not self-aware about it yet. Your numbers are give or take a month plus or minus 30%. One month you do like 200, 300 grand. The next month you're back down to like 100 grand. Sometimes it's that wild of a swing. Sometimes it's you hit 100K a month. And you stay there for like fucking what? A year? You know?
Sometimes you hit a number that's like $300,000, $400,000 a month. And you just can't fucking find a way to push past it. You think that you're trying. And to be fair, sometimes you do try new things. But you do them in such a risk-averse way. And I ask you a very simple thing. What forced you to grow when you had to grow? It was a back against the wall sensation. However, It's not healthy in any way, shape, or form to feel like your back's against the wall to continue to grow as you continue getting richer and richer. That's not what we want to recreate for our circumstances. That's going to create a toxic cycle. Where you have to tap back into that and almost self-sabotage to continue your growth from that point forward.
Does that make sense? We don't want to do that, but most of us can recognize that's typically when we see our most growth. I... over the last several years. have done one thing successfully again and again and again. to take my businesses and my clients' businesses. to substantially greater revenue numbers in short durations of time. And it's very simple. It all boils down to this. I push them to take a much bigger risk than what they're comfortable with in a given moment. All it comes down to. And I ask you this just to reflect real quick before I continue. When is the last time you took that level of risk? That would seriously. Not fucking destroy you. But would set you back a little bit in the instance it didn't work out for you. And you'd have to work back to that point to get there again. When's the last time you took that level of risk in your life? Anybody?
It's a measly $1,500 a month for you or two grand for those that joined later. Not a big, hairy investment. To be fair though to you, you were living at your mom's house at the time. Yeah, contextually that was, I understand. A big, big Big fucking risk. I'll give you an example. One of my clients, his name's Tom. Tom is the guy who sells the Section 8 real estate investing course for all those who have seen Tom. Probably making some type of controversial content that comes across your news feed. 100% of the time. I bet he's on live right now. Now, Tom. As you know, out there and as controversial as he is in most of his content. He does anywhere from about seven to 900K a month with his info products, specifically.
And he's been stuck there. You know how long he's been stuck there? Somebody guess. Longer. I told Tom to get into info products in 2021. Tom made an offer that was $500. to have an hour call with him. I was like, sick, I'll pay. I wanted to learn about Section A real estate. It sounded pretty badass. I got all my questions answered in the first 30 minutes. I asked him, 'Well, you have a course to sell me or something, you know?' He goes, 'No.' And I was like, 'Well, let me take the next 30 minutes telling you what you need to do, Mr. Tom.' Tom, within the following 72 hours, went out and created a course. He just filmed a fuckload of stuff. Action-oriented guy.
Tom did 700K his first month. Last month, he did 7,900K. That was in 2021. It's 2024 now. What happened? It's very simple. You know what Tom hasn't done? hadn't taken a big risk to grow that number further. What Tom does is the same thing every single month. And if you follow him, you know exactly what I'm talking about. He'll post dozens of pieces of content a day in some days, at least five to six on other days that are lower. He'll go live every fucking day on TikTok and Instagram. He does the Tai Lopez thing where he's got his little tripod and he'll do, you know, specifically Instagram. and TikTok for his live streaming services. That's the same thing. Join my webinar, join my webinar, join my webinar.
And guess what it does? Well, it does the same thing every month. So the same thing that he's been doing for three years, he's managed to sustain. The revenue that he did. But he hasn't managed to grow past it. His follower count has grown by a little bit. But that has to happen in order to continue. The seven to 900K coming in every month, doesn't it? You can't pitch the same people every month and expect the same result again and again and again. You have to get new people every month in order to sustain that level of result. Tom's attempted to advertise. You know how much Tom, who has a $20 million house? Tom, without exaggerating, has like four Rolls Royces that are all brand new, okay?
They're like $500,000 to $700,000 per car. He takes risks on things like that, like lifestyle stuff. He moved from a custom house he built in Wilmington, North Carolina, down here to Miami. He bought a $5 million yacht that cost him like $40K a month. You know what's uncomfortable for Tom? giving me more than like 30K a month to spend on ads. Is Tom too poor to afford more? No. No, he's not. Big money Tom can afford much more than $30,000 a month in ad spend. But here's the thing, that ad spend typically for Tom only produces like a two to one, maybe on the best months, like three to one ROI. Tom's producing, in his own mind, infinite money without any cost, besides the substantial amount of time that he puts into it, organically.
So you know what Tom hasn't done literally until this month? He's not taking a big fucking risk. That would grow his numbers. at least another million dollars a month greater than what they are now. because he's uncomfortable doing it. so just recently you know per usual like I typically have to do with some clients some clients It's instant. Some clients it takes like Tom three years to get it to finally crack. where I say, Tom. Are you tired? I'm making the same fucking amount of money every single month. He's like, yeah, you know, yeah, I'm finally ready. And I go to Tom's house on Monday. We're going to launch, without exaggerating, probably... 800 different individual pieces of content that we're going to run as engagement campaigns with just 100k a month towards engagement.
We'll spend 400k a week. I'm sorry, $400k in the month, $100k a week. So, $500k in total is what Tom's finally going to spend through the month of May on ads. What do you think a guy like me, who's taken 30 plus personal brands to over a million a month with just paid ads, is going to do with $500,000 in a month of ad spend? You think I'm gonna fuck that up? How much money do you think I'm going to flip 500K into? Throw a number. Thank you. Thank you. I mean, I'd be ashamed if it was only a million. You know? I guarantee you I could flip that $500,000 into at least $1. 5 million, $ 2 million in like a worst-case scenario. Now, although Tom's typically doing a two-to-one row as. Is turning $500,000 into $1. 5 more attractive than turning 30 into 60? Right? Now you know what Tom said to me? He said, 'This is very uncomfortable, Jeremy. Very, very, very uncomfortable.' I am not looking forward to these Amex bills.
What do you think he's going to say in 30 days? That's right. He's going to shake my fucking hand and hopefully give me a fat rev share check. But right now, how does he feel about it? Pretty uncomfortable, right? And the simplest way to articulate it to you. Tom's taking a big risk, right? He's taking half. A little more than half. of what he makes just off that business. HA! Of the cash flow. Risking it on ad spend. If that ends up making Tom, let's just say an extra million a month. He doubled his business in a month. Why was he able to double the business in a month? Because of a big fucking risk that's excessively uncomfortable to him, but it's not gonna destroy his life financially, it's not gonna put him into any kind of harm.
If he literally waited two months, he'd make the money back. If we didn't make a dollar. Right? So it's not going to destroy Tom, but he's not used to spending that amount of money on that. There's no technical material game that Tom gets out of that. Like, if he gets a yacht and he spends 500K on a down payment on a yacht, what's he get? Get your fucking yacht. You know? I put $98,000 down on that white Rolls Royce you see outside. I get a Rolls Royce out of it, right? If I put $98,000 into ad spend... I get nothing but stress and adrenaline until it turns into money again. Right? But which is technically better for me to spend the 98 grand on. Almost every single one of you in this room. Spend an excessive amount of money on lifestyle bullshit. And I guarantee you spend more on lifestyle than what you've done in business risk recently. That's the number one plague of this room.
Some of you frugal people are thinking in your head, 'Oh, well, Jeremy, I spend more on business stuff than I spend on lifestyle.' Yeah, but you're still not taking a risk that makes you uncomfortable. And that's the point of what I'm trying to articulate to you here. When and what. is going to occur in your life that makes you say, 'I gotta fucking make more money, like a lot more. Like in Tom's case. Double in a month.' Now this is the second half of this. I want you to really make sure you understand so it lands well. What's your reason for making more money right now? Like, if you needed to double your business within 30 days, truly, what is the reason you're going to take on that level of risk?
Is it just to make more money? Is that a good enough drive? Just to have a new number in your account that's a little higher. Is that why any of us make money? No. You all have your own individual drivers for what you like to make money for and what you then like to turn around and spend it on. Some of you are in wildly different financial situations than the others in this room. But you all have something that's big enough that you either want to buy, that you've been putting off, or that you need to afford in order to improve your life to the level that you want it to be at. And you've been pushing it off, or you've been trying to do the little game where you save to do it, or you've just been waiting for a windfall, lump sum.
To occur in your life to finally get that shit. I'll give you an example. I used to pay taxes on an annual basis. And. I'd say, like pre. 2020, I'd have like six figure. tax bills, like maybe like the a hundred to 500 K range. And then, every time after that, I've had to start putting up, like, low seven-figure or like very high six-figure tax bills. And I fucking hate it. I hate taxes so much I got hypnotized to like taxes, okay? And I still don't like them generally, but there's like a subconscious level of me that's okay with it. The point is, okay. Here's what I'm trying to say, okay? What my accountant did, he said, 'Hey, why don't you start paying some of this on a quarterly basis instead of on an annual basis so you don't feel as fucked at the end of the year.' And I was like, 'I'm not going to like it either way.' But here's the thing.
What it did was actually really good for me, and it led me to a pretty significant lesson I want to share with you. So my accountant told me, for this quarter so far, I was $700,000 in taxes. Okay? Without the pit in my stomach. And the nausea I felt right after hearing that. I then came to the conclusion, I was like, 'Oh, this is a great thing. Here's a great thing. I'm now driven. I just want to be very direct with you. Do I have $700,000 to pay the taxes? Yes. Could I just take $700,000 and pay the taxes? Yes. But is that the smartest thing for me to do? From a very frugal financially responsible perspective? Probably. Okay, to be fair.
However, that's not how I looked at it. I looked at it and said, 'How can I earn the $700,000? And how can I do it? In a very safe and sustainable way, where even if I failed, I could still take the money and pay it off anyway that I already have for it. So I determined. I was like, 'Okay. What is the big fucking risk I can take? That's going to potentially get me that $700,000. And for me, it was trading. Yeah, I decided to trade. I decided to essentially just gamble on the direction of a candle. For whether I was probable to get that $700,000 or not. And you know, when I did that? I did that a year ago on April 17th.
At the Q2 Mastermind last year, Sean comes in here saying he made like $400,000.' I thought to myself, no, no fucking way. Okay. And Sean's over here saying you could trade without using your own money with some like prop. for him. And I was like, bullshit, dude, no way. And you know, like $80,000 later on evaluation accounts to try to become like a funded trader. I actually got like, you know, funded accounts. And I started trading with them. And it took until 11 months after that. No, it was March. I got $199,000 from that skill. Now, it wasn't $700,000. thousand dollars, but it's good progress towards this. Now here's the thing, right?
I know I only risked $80,000 to be able to get to the point where I could, essentially, in total, so far, I've netted about $239,000 off of that. This month I'll get, well, not technically this month. It'll be next month. I'll get $104,000. In June, I'll get $104,000. In July, I'll get $104,000. And then in August, I can rip whatever amount I want. Like, I could actually get the $700,000 in August specifically. Now, here's my point, okay? For me, that was a big risk, and I'll tell you why. It was a time risk. I spent... at least an hour or two a day of make money attention that could have gone towards rev share clients on my agency, which would have made me far more money in that time frame.
But instead, I got paid an average of $13,000 a month net for that entire year of learning that new skill. A big fucking risk with the time that I could have spent making far more money elsewhere. It made me very uncomfortable. There were many times I thought I was getting scammed. There were many times I was prepared to send people to Sean's house and kick the shit out of him. There's a lot of instances where I felt this was not real and not probable to be playing out my way. And it did play out my way. And it's probable to get me to the point where I could actually produce that $700,000 off of it. I did. It's not a happy ending story.
I did have to pay the $700,000 with the money that I intended to initially pay it with. Not a good ending to it yet. But give me until about Q3, mastermind, I'll have a better outcome for you. Does this make sense? I want to be clear. Sometimes it's like Tom, where you're big money Tom, you've got the $500,000 to spend. You're going to spend it on lifestyle bullshit anyway. Why would you not take the risk? And then Tom finally comes to the conclusion it's worth his while. He's already spending a fuckload of time. In my world, I spend very little time producing revenue. Most of what I do is very high impact actions. short duration of time that make a lot of fucking money.
My dollar value per hour is typically in the thousands to tens of thousands on rev share deals. It made no sense for me to risk that time to take that action to learn a new skill set that could potentially make me that income. But I did. So there's two ways you take a big fucking risk. Either with a substantially greater amount of time towards that additional thing that you want to do, or with a substantially greater amount of revenue than what you're comfortable spending that will not financially destroy you if you do it. Does that make sense? Take 30 seconds. and I want you to pull the answer out of you. What could you spend the money on? Or what could you spend the time on that you know with a high degree of certainty, if you fucking did it. And have a high probability to play out the way that you'd want it to. Take 30 seconds.
Something big. What'd you come up with? Tchau, tchau. What? I don't know. By the dip, okay? What do you come up with? With three hours following this. Got it. What did you come up with? Yeah.
Why is that a weak answer? That's a good answer. You ready to do it? Do you already do it?
In the next 30 days. And I was thinking about spending like, easily, I don't know, 5, 10K a month. And so, that would work so comfortably. So now I'm like, what do I look like if I spent, I don't know, 15, 20, 25? I don't know. James. I had double ad spend right away and buy a house and a couch. Good. Now, to be clear, I love the house one specifically and the ad spend, especially if you're considering doing it at the same time because it stretches you a bit, right? It gets you to the point where you're like, 'Fuck, I gotta, I got to make more, you know? Because when you see that number go down, here's the most important part from what I've experienced.
What's the sweet spot in your bank account that makes you feel desperate? You know exactly what the fuck I'm talking about. What's the number? Where, when it hit that level, you instantly. Instantly we're like, 'Holy shit.' I have no other option right now but to work my fucking ass off. You experienced it when you were broke. You experienced it as a rich person. You know exactly what the fuck I'm talking about. When's the last time you even got near that number? And tapped into that energy. The number changes. What I like to encourage, and this is very important to understand, is you stay generally slightly above the sweet spot. So you don't tap into desperation, but you tap into like that level of energy of 'I'm getting fucking close to the sweet spot.' And that pushes you.
It's like a slingshot. Every time I get close, it's like I go higher every single time after. And I take the money, shove it into a bunch of investment accounts or take risks with it, spend it on ad spend, turns back into more money. Pull it right back down, right above the sweet spot, you know, fucking release it again. By doing so, I'm not in any way, shape, or form encouraging you to spend all the money on a bunch of bullshit lifestyle stuff. I'm encouraging... you to take risks, invest, set the money in places that, simply put, you just can't tap into easily. Something like, as an example, equity markets, real estate, ad space. Things that are very probable to turn that money into more money, but you still have access to the money technically, but it's not in your main account that you actually look at every day for where and what we're considering the sweet spot to be.
Does that make sense? We all have an operating account. That you look at. That determines how hard you're going to work today. And you know exactly what I'm talking about. When that number got big enough, you stopped working as hard. Does that make sense? So in James's point, I really want to emphasize this. A house and ad spend, I bet, would take James's account down to a number where he's going to be more tapped into that sales team. Than he is right now. He's gonna be more tapped into making better ads than he is right now. He's gonna be more tapped into those marketing analytics than he is right now. He's gonna be locked in to ensure that that money turns into more money. In comparison to what he is right now.
Right now, he's doing good. But is he doing as great as he possibly could? No. And it's because he's not coming close to the sweet spot, which is the third thing I want to make sure you walk away with as an emphasis. Okay? The sweet spot. That is the fucking glory position to be in where you are typically the most driven. Does it make sense why? It's not like you're actually poor or operating at that level. It's a level, and everybody's different. I'll give you an example with mine to be very transparent with you. If I get to about 200K in my operating account, I get to a point where I'm like, 'Man, I got to get that way up there, you know?' And if I get to the point where I'm at like 150K in the operating account, I'm like, 'I can't even take as big of a risk right now.' Because I only have fucking 150K in the operating account, you know?
And to be clear, I invest about $200,000 every month into the S&P 500 and into about half, the other half goes towards the big tech stocks and just the other big half of the S&P. I take a good chunk of money every month. I reinvest it into ad spend. I buy Section 8 properties. Simply put, I consistently reinvest. And then, give or take a month, like 10% to 15% of what I spend, I spend on bullshit lifestyle stuff just so I feel good about making the money that I make. Now to be clear, that's typically once again what pulls me back down. And then the next month it comes back up. And then I pull it back down. And then I'm consistently tapping into the drive.
Does that make sense to you? Give me another example of the big risk. What's yours? That's a particular team member I'm hiring right now, and they're going to cost double to pay this particular team member. I'm going to let it go, but this is an A player I'm hiring, and that's just going to make me way more money than trying to pay. How do you feel when you put up, how much did you put in the S &P so far this year? Two and two. How did you feel when you lump summed? A good amount into there. What did it do to you mentally? Um, Level of abundance, in a way. When you invested it, was it the excess you invested, or did it pull you back down to that sweet spot in your operating account and make you work harder after?
Or was it just pure excess you invested? It was a little bit of both— a little bit of both. I want to encourage you to make sure you really understand this in detail. I don't want you to just pull from the excess. I want you to pull slightly in. That's where the uncomfortability is, to be clear. That's where you interpret the uncomfortability at— is when you pull it right below that number of where it's no longer excess and it's into money that you wanted to do X, Y, Z with. And it makes you once again just feel like, 'I don't know if I should do that,' you know? Does that make sense? Once again, every single time, and this is the most important part, just as a little caveat of this whole thing, I'm under the assumption every single one of you in this room right now are under the mental frame of...
I... I'm an earner. I have proven to myself again and again and again that I can make back what I spend rapidly. When is the last time you spent in excess of money and could not make it back rapidly? I can't even remember for me. Okay? And that reiterates to me, I can take these big risks without it actually being a big risk. It just takes me into a number in my operating account where I'm like, 'FUCK!' A little more than I wanted to spend. Because I like to keep it at that number, which is purely psychological. There's no actual reason to keep it at that number outside of the comfort that I get psychologically from being at that number in the account. Does that make sense to you?
Let it sink in. Hiring a new staff member, that's what? Double what you pay a current staff? It's not gonna put you in that fucking number. You have to take a bigger risk than that to see bigger growth. That's just a smart move to make. That's not a big risk. That's just a good fucking business move. There's a huge difference between what we're talking about and an example like that. Big risk, substantial. Do you think Tom hiring a new staff member is like, dude, $500,000 in a month is what the fuck I'm talking about? Does that make sense? Like, put the house on the line. You know what this does to you when you take this action? Here's the result.
When you come out the other side of it, what do you think Tom's mentality is going to be like when he comes out the other side of spending $500,000 in a month and it turns into a million, a million five, or two million? How the fuck do you think Tom's going to feel after that? Think about it, tap into it right now with me. Act like you're Tom. You're gonna walk around feeling like the fucking, you're gonna feel like a god with money. You're gonna be like, 'I have the Midas touch.' It's going to validate you to a whole new level, an extreme level that you don't feel right now. You know you can turn money into more money. You know you can.
But you're not willing to risk your own money to do it at a greater level than what you currently do? For what? The extreme level of exuberance you will feel. The certainty you will feel to then do it again. Do you know what it does for you? Makes you grow substantially faster in whatever pace you're at now. You know, we've talked about this before in this group. Does anybody remember what their COVID numbers were? I know some of you got absolutely slaughtered and your businesses completely went under. But listen to this. Some of you during COVID grew so fast and so rapidly. Like your year-over-year business growth might have been this. And then during COVID, you experienced excessive growth. Like for me, as an example, I was in the info product niche.
Every client I had at that time, generally in the info product space, every single client was up like 400% for the year. Literally the lowest client year-over-year growth was like 83%. Every single person made so much money than what they were regularly used to. How do you think they felt after when things went back to like 30-ish percent year over year again? Is 100% year-over-year growth. Is that possible for you? Probably not if you keep taking the weak fucking risks you do right now. You know what gets substantial, sustainable growth year over year? Big risks. Big risks. If you in a year... had a moment like this. When we look at Tom's revenue chart, at the end of 2024. And in that month, between April and May, of dipping 500K down, and it turning into more money after.
What will his chart look like? You think he'll do it again after? How much more will Tom's business grow from taking a risk like that? You know, there's another risk I told Tom to take. I said, 'Tom. I got a guy. You could pay them about 250 grand. He'll get you on every big podcast. You will see no direct way to track. Whether this pays you back or not. You have no clue whether it turns back into 250K or more. But every single time. Every single time. I had a big client do it. creates fucking monster growth because you become a household name temporarily.' And then we take those clips and we run them as engagement campaigns in perpetuity, and we maintain that level of familiarity with people.
But we have no idea whether the 250K technically paid off or not. You know what Tom said about that one? He's like, 'I'm not there yet.' Thank you. I want to do that one. And that's fair because ads. I can track whether people are going to convert off that 500K spent, right? But that's the next level for Tom, outside of just spending more and more money on ads month over month, right? I wanna be clear when I say this. There's technically two risks on the table that are pretty big for Tung. One has a more direct way to track back whether it worked or not. If you have options out of all the risks that you might have considered taking here today, go with the one that you can track a little better, that you at least have a higher level of certainty with.
Because that'll make you... ease into it and actually do it. Does that make sense? The one that's a little more loose. That you can't directly measure. Save that one for later when it's the apparent one to take. That make sense? I want to be very direct in a few bullet points and caveats to this. Financial responsibility. I'm going to give you a negative example of this. So as I mentioned last year, Sean comes in here and says, 'Oh, I'm doing 400 K to like a million a month with trading.' Okay. And there's a bunch of people in here, obviously, that were like. Fucking sounds sick, dude. Tom, I'm sorry, Sean's pitch was. He takes 20 minutes a day. You know, I'm in and out. Yeah, yeah. And.
Look this is, this is. It's a breeze, man. Just when it hits this line, you know, you do this. And there's a bunch of people here who took risks. And that decided to either invest in Sean as a coach or just bet the house on, you know, trading as a hopeful strategy. I won't say the name of this person out of respect. They're not here today. However, There's one inner circle student in particular. That. was making a lot of money in the business, but they weren't actually able to pull a lot of money out of the business. They had loans that did not enable them to take above a certain salary number every year from these loans. They're called covenants. Covenants are like restrictions on what you can and can't do in your business financially.
Until you pay the loan back. So this guy, he's making a fuckload of money in his business. but he's only making like a couple hundred grand a year take home. Because of the bank covenants. So. He hears Sean come in here and say, 'Can make all this money trading.' And that was like the light at the end of the tunnel for this guy. Because we're talking— probably five years down the road— before he'd gain control of his business again after that loans paid off. So he hears about trading and he's like, 'Fuck yeah, dude, that sounds sweet.' So he does two things. He invested in Sean as a mentor. And he invests about a hundred grand. Which was the last hundred grand that he had.
Into these things called evaluation accounts, trying to pass them so then he could trade real money and actually get paid real money. You know what happened to this guy? Do you think it worked out? You think there's a fairytale ending to this thing? Get a text from this guy one day. Hey, I got to drop out of the inner circle. I can't afford it. I'm like, dog, your business does seven figures a month. What the fuck are you talking about? You made an extra like 100K a month in the first month of being a part of the group. What are you talking about? And he explains all this stuff to me about the trading thing and the covenants on the bank restrictions and all this. And I'm like, what the fuck?
Why would you do that? And you... Seemed hopeful. You know? I thought that it would work out. The guy had to downgrade where he was living to a less expensive place. He had two cars, now he's got one car. The guy had to like, it's a back against the wall situation for sure. After the downgrades, he fucking goes and starts taking action on a secondary business to get him some cash flow he can actually control. He obviously stops trading completely because that didn't fucking work for him. Is that the kind of risk that I'm talking about of literally spending every dollar you have on taking a risk? Everybody say it with me. No. No. Okay? That's not what I'm talking about. Okay?
I'm not telling you to take the entirety of your account and spend it on a risk. Are we all on the same page with that? Don't do that. Okay? Yeah? I am encouraging. That you take a risk that does make you uncomfortable. Okay? Not broke, uncomfortable. Very far apart between those two things. Does that make sense? Notice what I said about my risk. Hey, I got the money to pay the tax. I'm not going to touch the money that I'm going to pay the taxes with. I'm going to try other things with other money. And time in my case. To try to generate this 700k so I could pay it with that instead of that. Does that make sense? I did not dip into the 700k that was there to pay the tax.
Make sense? Responsible risk. Okay? It's the underlying premise, so I want to make sure you understand. So nobody ends up like that student I just talked about. You don't need to defend yourself. Everything's fine. It's not your fault. Nobody blames you. Nobody blames Sean for that. It was not Sean's fault. I did, I got a Rolls Royce from it, and it was profitable, it was a great risk, and I'll make far more money from it. It was smart, not dumb. Okay, to be clear. It's very important you walk away with this as like a summary lesson. If you choose to not take that big risk. Why in the fuck would you not?
What would make you not do it?
Pull it out now. If you go to take the risk and something comes up inside of you. That makes you say, 'I don't know,' you know, 'I could do this instead.' And fucking deal with it. Because to be clear, here's what you're in for if you don't. What happens when you don't take the big risks is very simple. Thank you. Your growth chart. It just looks like this. That's all. Can I just continue your slight uptrend? You know? Let me ask you a question. How fast did this last year pass by for you? You know what's pretty crazy for me? I remember watching the NBA Finals last year because the Miami Heat were in it. Playoffs just started again. I'm like, 'What the fuck?' It's already here again?
I turned 30 at the end of the month. I gotta keep it real, I did really well in my 20s, I feel great about it, you know? Fucking crushed my 20s. But I'm like, holy fuck, dude, I'm gonna be 30! Whoa! You know how fast time goes by as you continue to get older? I've told you this equation before, but think about it like this. When you're one year old, how much is one year of your life to you, perceptually? It's a whole entire lifetime. Yeah, it's the entire lifetime, right? On a fraction, it's one over one. As soon as you turn two years old, how much is one year of your life at the age of two? It's 1 over 2. When you turn 50 years old, what's the fraction?
1 over 50. Every year that continues to go by becomes less and less, and less, and less, and less significant, which is what makes time feel like it goes faster and faster and faster and faster. Just math. Does that make sense? Literally every year you live, it will continue to go by faster and faster and faster than what it is right now, which is to your benefit, believe it or not. Yeah. Kind of sucks, you know? Nobody here really wants to die, I'm sure. Hopefully. However, at the end of the day, it's technically to your benefit, right? Because at the same time, when we make big moves, when we consistently invest, when we're consistent with this type of behavior, I mean, it all compounds into our favor much faster every year that we continue to live.
If you don't take these actions, it fucks you faster at a faster compounded rate. Did you get that? So all I'm saying is, look— If this is what you want to maintain over the next several decades, just keep doing the little shit you're doing now. You know, little moves. Oh, I don't need ads. Oh, how many people have you DM'd? You know? Oh, I don't need to pitch people, I do this. Okay, great, yeah, awesome. Well, here's what happens to the other people that actually take risks. The people who take risks, they kind of look like this, and then they wake the fuck up, and they're like, 'Oh,' and then they go up here, and then it's, 'Oh, you know?' And every year of their life, by the time that you're wherever the fuck you end at and where they end at, they're at a substantially greater level financially than where you are.
They're the people who end up with all the shit that you've wanted your entire life that you don't have because you didn't do these levels of risks consistently throughout your business career. When is the best time to take an action like that? Today. Now, as soon as possible, when you're in a level of comfort. Be honest. Be brutally honest with yourself. How many of you are truly comfortable? And you're just maintaining the consistent growth. Put your hand up. Be honest with yourself. Don't bullshit yourself. How many of you... are comfortable. You're maintaining. You know you want more. But you're not really putting in substantial levels of work to get them more. That's how most people end up. They just... Maintain. They're growing. But they're maintaining. Does that make sense? That's the whole point of what I'm trying to talk to you about here today. Fuck maintaining. Makes no sense to only maintain. Maintaining is one part of the game. The other big part of the game that probably got you to this point where you're at today. Is there something you did when you were poorer than you are right now? That was a big risk to you that pushed you to the point you're at today. Does that make sense? Anybody got questions on this? Anything. Okay, cool.
